Mathematics, Engineering, Science Achievement (MESA) Program
Phone: 253-566-5155
Email: ascarcella@tacomacc.edu
Website: https://www.tacomacc.edu/academics-programs/academic-support/mesa
MESA supports STEM (science, technology, engineering, mathematics) students who are first-generation, low-income, and/or people of color pursing STEM majors to
transfer successfully to earn their bachelor’s degrees in STEM. Support includes:
         •   Academic and transfer advising




                                                                                                                                                                   24 / 478
Tacoma Community College



          •   Academic enrichment
          •   Scholarship and internship information
          •   Industry and social connections
          •   Volunteer and community opportunities
          •   Professional development
          •   Book and calculator loans
          •   Study center with computers




Gig Harbor Campus Learning Resource Center
Phone: 253-460-2424
Website: https://www.tacomacc.edu/about/campuses/gig-harbor-campus/gig-harbor-campus
TCC’s Gig Harbor Campus Learning Resource Center provides a student computer lab, tutoring, and other resources including transfer materials, tutoring handouts, and
scholarship information.

Student Support Services

Personal Support Services
Phone:253-460-3920
Email: foodpantry@tacomacc.edu
Website: https://www.tacomacc.edu/tcc-life/campus-services/food-pantry
The Max & Margi Harned Titan Food Pantry is open to all TCC students. It provides food to get you through the day and food for home to fill in your budget. We also offer
resource referrals to services in the community that can help with food insecurity. The Nourish Mobile Food Bank is on TCC's Tacoma Campus every Tuesday from 4:30 to
6:30 pm in parking lot F.



Early Learning Center
Phone: 253-566-5180
Website: https://www.tacomacc.edu/tcc-life/around-campus/early_learning_center
The Early Learning Center is a state certified, accredited, nonprofit campus learning center (Bldg. 3) that offers a high-quality, affordable child care program designed to
enable parents to pursue education at TCC by providing a safe, nurturing environment for their children. Additional services provided by the Center include breakfasts, hot
lunches and afternoon snacks; developmental screening assessment; and family resources and referrals. TCC students are given priority for enrolling their children. Spaces
are also available to children of TCC staff and faculty.
The Center accepts children from one year to five years of age and provides a comprehensive program of activities suited to the developmental level of the children. Tuition
is determined by a sliding fee scale, based on parent’s income. Eligible three and four-year-olds can enroll in the preschool ECEAP program which provides 6½ hours of free
preschool per day. Also, eligible children one year to three years of age may enroll in the Early Head Start program.



Safe Zone and The Rainbow Action Committee
Website: https://www.tacomacc.edu/tcc-life/lgbtq-resources
TCC is committed to creating a safe and supportive environment for our students and employees who are part of the LGBTQ+ community. The Rainbow Action Committee,
housed within TCC’s Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Council (EDIC), is a dedicated a group of employees deeply invested in supporting all of our LGBTQ+ students, employees,
and community members.
If you need links and resources to support you, we have a public-facing LGBTQ+ resource page on the TCC website. We regularly connect with other TCC departments and
Tacoma community groups to keep our information up to date; however, if you have additional resources or ideas for the page, please connect with us by contacting the
advocates listed on the webpage.
The Rainbow Action Committee also advocates for the rights and increased inclusion of members of the LGBTQ+ community on campus. We survey students and employees
regularly to find out what matters to YOU and what challenges you may have experienced at TCC. For example, recently with the support of TCC leadership, the committee
successfully designated all-gender restrooms in every building on campus (you can find a map of these restrooms on our LGBTQ+ resource page). Finally, one of the Rainbow
Action Committee’s highest priorities is our Safe Zone program. Safe Zone is a campus-wide initiative to make sure that TCC is a safe and empowering environment for
LGBTQ+ students and employees. Starting Spring Quarter of 2022, Safe Zone will offer ongoing trainings, workshops, and seminars available to everyone at TCC. Safe Zone’s
LGBTQ+ related topics will include how different aspects of a person’s identity intersect with their sexual orientation, gender identity, and gender expression. We want our
LGBTQ+ students and employees to be supported and heard at TCC. To learn more, get involved, and let us hear from you. Click here to learn more about Safe Zone and
LGBTQ+ Resources at TCC.

ACCT&202 - Principles of Accounting II

General Information
ACCT&202 is a continuation of ACCT&201. Introduces accounting for long-term
debt and investments. Examines the measuring and reporting of Stockholders'
Equity and consolidated statements. Re-emphasizes the preparation of the
Statement of Cash Flows. Introduction to methods used to interpret financial
statements.

Adult Basic Education Department
TCC’s Adult Basic Education (ABE) program offers an affordable pathway for students to achieve their professional and educational aspirations. Students can earn their high
school diploma through the High School+ program, take GED® support classes, or take classes to improve their reading, writing, and math skills to prepare for college level
courses.


For information on our Adult Basic Education department: https://www.tacomacc.edu/academics-programs/basic-education/
Building 7 Advising Area
Front Office: 253-566-5144
For general program questions email basicskills@tacomacc.edu
Adult Basic Education Department Chair: Allison Muir - amuir@tacomacc.edu

Programs
Adult Basic Education

Overview
Full Credential Title
Adult Basic Education

Catalog Description
The Basic Education for Adults (BEdA) department is an affordable and supported
pathway to college. BEdA offers a variety of programs for individuals who wish to
improve their basic reading, writing, math, conversation, and employment skills.
BEdA students receive comprehensive career pathway planning and navigation
support from Transitional Studies Navigators and Advisors. Programs include
English as a Second Language, Adult Basic Education, and High School Completion,
vocationally focused basic skills programs, and programs designed for
college-bound and Career Training participants.

Schedules include both day and evening classes. Students between the ages of
16-18 must obtain formal high school releases. Individuals with F-1, M-1, and J-1
visas are not eligible for federally funded Adult Basic Skills services. Before
enrolling, students are required to attend an information session and complete a
skills assessment.

                                                                                                                                                             60 / 478
Tacoma Community College




Courses
AH100 - Introduction to Allied Health

General Information
This course provides the student with an introduction to allied health professions
and the delivery of healthcare services. This course also prepares students for the
demands and challenges presented in many allied health programs by providing
strategies for success in the classroom, lab, library, and the clinical environment as
well as provide sound advice and guidance for maintaining their emotional and
physical well-being.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    3




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Course Requirements

  Recommended Preparation
  Type
  Prerequisite

    English
    Earn a minimum Grade of C in the following:
            • ENGL&101 - English Composition I


  Additional Comments:
  or concurrent enrollment in ENGL&101




AH200 - Infection Prevention and Control for Allied
Health Professionals

General Information
This course introduces the student to the fundamental concepts of infection
control and prevent the spread of infection within the healthcare setting. Students
will be introduced concepts related to the chain of infection and standard and
transmission-base precautions; discuss factors that affect the quality of an
effective infection prevention program; and presents an overview of pathogenic
organisms that may be transmitted in healthcare settings.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    5




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

  Type
  Prerequisite

    English

    Earn a minimum Grade of C in the following:
            • ENGL&101 - English Composition I

  Multicultural Course (5 credits)
  Type
  Completion Requirement

   Multicultural Course
   A single course may be used to satisfy the multicultural requirement, one
   specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement. Please
   consult the listings under each distribution area requirement to identify
   courses that fulfill both criteria.

   For the Anthropology Specialization, students may apply a single
   Multicultural-designated course from the Humanities Distribution or
   Social Sciences Distribution to fulfill up to three requirements
   simultaneously - multicultural requirement, one specialization
   requirement, and one distribution requirement.


   This overlap does not reduce the total degree requirement of 90 quarter
   credits.

   Earn at least 5 credits from the following:
            • Multicultural Courses




   Recommended Courses:
   ANTH&100 Survey of Anthropology - Survey of Anthropology
   (Anthropology Specialization Core Course, Social Science Distribution, &
   Multicultural Course)
   ANTH&206 Cultural Anthropology - Cultural Anthropology (Anthropology
   Specialization Core Course, Social Science Distribution, & Multicultural Course)
   ANTH&207 Linguistic Anthropology - Linguistic Anthropology
   (Anthropology Specialization Core Course, Humanities Distribution or Social
   Sciences Distribution & Multicultural Course)
   ANTH&210 Indigenous Peoples of North America - Indigenous Peoples of
   North America (Anthropology Elective Course, Social Sciences Distribution &
   Multicultural Course)

General Information                                                                General Information
A survey of the subfields of physical anthropology, archaeology, anthropological   Introduction to the study of the prehistory of humankind as revealed by material
linguistics and sociocultural anthropology through the examination of selected     remains. Covers excavation techniques, analyzing and dating artifacts, and a survey
problems in human biological and cultural evolution. An introduction to the        of world prehistory from the beginning of culture to the appearance of writing.
discipline of anthropology that asks what it means to be human. (Multicultural

ANTH&207 - Linguistic Anthropology

General Information
Introduction to linguistic methods and theories used within anthropology. Topics
include the structure of language, anatomy and evolution of language, the
relationship between language and culture, the study of language variation and of
language change. The linguistic database in the course is both historical and
cross-cultural.

Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5

ANTH299 - Independent Study

General Information
A variable credit (1-5) course based on independent study contracted between an
instructor and a student. The independent study will provide students an
opportunity to pursue an anthropological area of interest in depth beyond what
regular courses can provide. The emphasis may be field research, scholarly
research, or a combination.

Components                 Credits                     Max Credits
Lecture                    1                           5




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Course Requirements

  Enrollment Requirements
  Type
  Prerequisite

    Anthropology

    Earn a minimum Grade of B+ in the following:
            • ANTH&100 - Survey of Anthropology

    and Instructor permission and a written contract between the instructor,
    student and instructional administrator, describing the nature and method
    of the independent study.

  Multicultural Course (5 credits)
  Type
  Completion Requirement

    Multicultural Course

    A single course may be used to satisfy the multicultural requirement, one
    specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement. Please
    consult the listings under each distribution area requirement to identify
    courses that fulfill both criteria.

    For the Art Specialization, students may apply a single
    Multicultural-designated course from the Humanities Distribution to fulfill
    up to three requirements simultaneously - multicultural requirement, one
    specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement.


    This overlap does not reduce the total degree requirement of 90 quarter
    credits.

    Earn at least 5 credits from the following:
             • Multicultural Courses

    Recommended Courses:
    ART&100 Art Appreciation - Art Appreciation (Art Specialization Foundation
    Course, Humanities Distribution & Multicultural Course)
    CHIN&121 Chinese I - Chinese I (Humanities Distribution & Multicultural
    Course)
    JAPN&121 Japanese I - Japanese I (Humanities Distribution & Multicultural
    Course)
    SPAN&121 Spanish I - Spanish I (Humanities Distribution & Multicultural
    Course)

availability, and program requirements are subject to change and transfer credit(s)
may change your individualized academic plan. Click here to connect with an
advisor for this degree: Creative Arts, Literature, and Communication Pathway

ART102 - Two-Dimensional Design                                                           Completion Requirement
                                                                                          Additional Comments:
General Information                                                                       No Enrollment Requirements
This course is intended to introduce students to the basic elements of design in
two-dimensional space. Using a series of progressive projects, assignments will
feature basic formal elements and how they relate to graphic art. The organizing
compositional principles of proportion, movement, tension, balance, rhythm, and
harmony will be analyzed in examples of art. Discussions concerning line, plane,        ART104 - Introduction to Digital Art
form, texture, color, time, and shape are studied individually and in combination for
the final project. A student’s individual cultural background will be a resource for    General Information
inspiration. Students can expect to work about eight hours each week outside of         This course introduces students to the fundamentals of digital art, including design
class on their project assignments. Recommend prior to Graphic Design,                  principles, digital file management, and output techniques. Through hands-on
Printmaking, Photography, and Painting classes. Performance/skills course.              projects using Adobe Photoshop and Illustrator, students will explore the
                                                                                        possibilities of raster and vector graphics while developing essential computing
Components                                 Credits
                                                                                        skills such as layers, selection tools, and editing techniques. The course also covers
Lecture, Lab                               5
                                                                                        best practices for preparing digital work for accurate hardcopy printing. This
                                                                                        course is strongly recommended for students interested in digital art careers
                                                                                        including graphic design, animation, and video production.

                                                                                       84 / 478
Tacoma Community College




Courses
ASTR&110 - The Solar System

General Information
Study of the structure and composition of the Solar System. Topics discussed
include the history of Astronomy, the appearance of the nighttime sky, orbital
motion, gravity, the nature of light, theories of Solar System formation, and the
characteristics of the planets, moons, asteroids, and comets that inhabit the Solar
System. Laboratory included.

Components                                Credits
Lecture, Lab                              5




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Course Requirements

  Enrollment Requirements
  Type
  Completion Requirement

  Additional Comments:
  No Enrollment Requirements




ASTR&115 - Stars, Galaxies and the Cosmos

General Information
Study of the structure and composition of the universe including stars and galaxies.
Topics discussed include the theories of the formation of stars, galaxies, and the
universe; the nature of light and telescopes; the characteristics of the Sun; the
cycle of star formation, life and death; the types of stars and galaxies; and the
application of physical principles to making astronomical measurements.
Laboratory included.

Components                                Credits
Lecture, Lab                              5




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Course Requirements

  Enrollment Requirements
  Type
  Completion Requirement
  Additional Comments:
  No Enrollment Requirements

BIOL&100 - Survey of Biology                                                              BIOL&175 - Human Biology w/Lab

General Information                                                                       General Information
An introduction to biological principles. Topics include: scientific method; scientific   This course is a one-quarter class of human anatomy and physiology. This course
literacy; basic cellular anatomy and biochemical processes; evolution and genetics;       offers a brief overview of the human body for the non-science major. It covers
diversity of life; and ecology and environmental issues. Laboratory included.             some basics of chemistry and cells and then outlines all the major systems of the
                                                                                          human body. Laboratory included.

BIOL&160 - General Cell Biology

General Information
An introductory cell biology course primarily for students preparing for allied
health professions. Major concepts of cell biology will be introduced, including the
chemistry of life, the structure, reproduction, and metabolism of cells, genetics,
and evolutionary biology. Lab included. Recent high school chemistry or
introductory-level college chemistry is recommended before taking this course.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture, Lab                               5

BIOL179 - Special Topics in Biology

General Information
This topic course will explore one varying topic in biology. The specific biological
topic will be explored using a variety of methods which may include lectures,
discussions, seminars, research projects, field work, laboratory work, or all-day to   BIOL294 - Biology Research
overnight field trips. Example topics include: genetics, conservation biology, the
history of life, or other current topics in biology.                                   General Information
                                                                                       Students will work as part of a research team on a specific research project for the
Components                                 Credits
                                                                                       quarter. The course will involve either laboratory or field study depending on the
Lecture                                    2
                                                                                       project, evaluation of primary research papers relevant to the project, and
                                                                                       presentation of project results.

Botany Department
For more information on our Environmental Science department: https://www.tacomacc.edu/academics-programs/programs/environmentalscience
Environmental Science Department Chair: Kristen Harrison - kharrison@tacomacc.edu

Courses
BOT101 - General Botany

General Information
Presents basic concepts of plant biology to non-science majors including plant
characteristics, biodiversity, growth, reproduction, and ecology. Students discuss
current topics in agriculture, horticulture, medicine, ethnobotany, biotechnology,
ecology, conservation, and environmental issues. Labs include lab experiments,
greenhouse projects, field trips, and habitat restoration work in the TCC Nature
Area.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture, Lab                               5




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Course Requirements

  Recommended Preparation
  Type
  Prerequisite

    Math
    Proficiency in Algebra.




BOT179 - Special Topics in Botany

General Information
This course will explore various topics in plant biology using a variety of methods,
including seminars, laboratory experiments, lectures, research projects,
presentations, field work, and all-day or overnight field trips depending on the
quarter offered. Topics will vary by quarter. A course may include topics such as
ethnobotany, plant taxonomy, etc.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    2

That Associate in Applied Science in Business provides a training option in two
focused areas: Global Logistics and Entrepreneurship and Small Business

Management . The program provides essential skills in customer service,
marketing, and managing employees. Students will learn how to improve                   Fulfill ANY of the following requirements:
interpersonal communication, become a leader and motivators for employees and            Microsoft Word & Excel Course
peers, solve problems creatively, and make effective decisions. This training may be
                                                                                         Earn a minimum grade of C in the following:
used to develop knowledge and skills that will help students be competitive for a
                                                                                                     •    CU105 - Word I & Excel I
new job, or job retention and advancement in a current position.

TCC’s Business program leads to an Associate in Applied Sciences degree. The
                                                                                                                              OR
program’s structure is progressive, leading to three certificates for increasing
levels of job responsibility. Three specialized certificates are also available.

Human Resource Specialist Certificate

Overview
Full Credential Title
Human Resource Specialist Certificate

Catalog Description
The Human Resource Specialist Certificate prepares students for careers in human
resources by building essential skills in employee relations, recruitment,
compliance, and organizational development. This certificate is ideal for individuals
already in the workforce who are returning to school to enhance their HR
knowledge. Courses in this certificate may be applied toward the Associate in
Applied Science (AAS) in Business.

BUS&201 - Business Law

General Information
An introduction to the American legal system and the functions of law in a business
environment; legal reasoning and the process of resolving disputes in society; a
preliminary analysis of contractual arrangements and business association in the
business community.

descriptive techniques, probability, estimation, hypothesis testing, regression,
correlation and related concepts.

This course covers the behavioral and technical aspects of managing projects. The      This course covers advanced project management topics necessary for
course will explore various approaches for effectively managing team dynamics,         implementation of and excellence in project management. The course will provide
project planning, monitoring, and controlling activities. Students gain insight into   a framework for approaching, evaluating, and implementing project risk and
project leadership and team management. The goal is to prepare students with the       quality management in order to obtain optimal project results by identifying and
necessary knowledge related to effective project team management as well as            applying relevant quality management tools, activities, and methods for achieving
project cost, quality, and performance in order to satisfy the business objectives     systemic quality management and quality improvement across all phases of the
and successfully manage the implementation of a project. Project management            project life cycle. The course will also explore agile project management
software will be used to support the implementation of a project. Students will        methodologies. Best practices in the management of international projects, human
prepare a project plan at an intermediate level of difficulty.                         resource management, risk management, project leadership, quality and
                                                                                       communications management will be explored and discussed.

BUS462 - Capstone: Human Resource Management

General Information
This culminating course is the capstone course for students with studies
concentrated in human resource management. Students will evaluate case studies
and integrate all human resource management knowledge and skills learned in
previous courses including critical strategies in the areas of legal/regulatory
compliance, recruitment and selection of personnel, performance and feedback
mechanisms, and financial and benefits compensation.

Components                 Credits                     Max Credits
Lecture                    4                           5

CHEM&262 - Organic Chemistry w/Lab II

General Information
Organic chemistry for science majors. Topics include: structure, properties and
reactions of alkenes, alkynes, alcohols, radicals, and aromatic compounds;
introduction to NMR, UV/VIS, and mass spectroscopy. Laboratory included.

Components                                Credits
Lecture, Lab                              5

CHEM&263 - Organic Chemistry w/Lab III

General Information
Organic chemistry for science majors. Topics include: structure, properties and
reactions of aldehydes, ketones, amines, and carboxylic acids and their derivatives;
introduction to biological molecules and biochemical processes. Laboratory
included.

                                                                                                                                                                  143 / 478
Tacoma Community College




Courses
COL101 - College 101

General Information
To facilitate students' development of the capacities necessary to confidently
navigate their unique college and career pathways. Topics may include:
self-advocacy skills; leveraging students cultural capital; setting and achieving
goals; academic planning; self care and stress management; cultivating mentor,
peer and community relationships; communication; career exploration; financial
education; connecting with college and community resources; critical thinking
related to equity, diversity, and inclusion; and information literacy.

Components                                  Credits
Lecture                                     5

  Multicultural Course (5 credits)
  Type
  Completion Requirement

   Multicultural Course

   A single course may be used to satisfy the multicultural requirement, one
   specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement. Please
   consult the listings under each distribution area requirement to identify
   courses that fulfill both criteria.

   For the Communication Studies Specialization, students may apply a single
   Multicultural-designated course from the Communication Skills or
   Humanities Distribution to fulfill up to three requirements simultaneously
   - multicultural requirement, one specialization requirement, and one
   distribution requirement.

   This overlap does not reduce the total degree requirement of 90 quarter
   credits.

            • Multicultural Courses

CMST&240 - Intercultural Communication

General Information
A survey course of human interaction across cultures, focusing on values,
behaviors, and perceptions to develop empathy and adaptability in diverse
communication scenarios. We critically analyze culture, media, power dynamics,
and more, challenging stereotypes and discriminatory practices. Students
investigate intersections of social identities and historical movements, promoting
equity, representation, and social justice. Additionally, students conduct ethical
research following APA format guidelines for credibility and reliability.
Throughout, we integrate perspectives from local, U.S., and global cultures.

Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5

                                                                                      General Information
General Information                                                                   This course aims to provide a broad introduction to provider-patient
This course covers an introduction to the communication dynamics of                   communication, mass communication, health journalism, corporate
organizations and the role of communication in the professional and management        communication, communication by non-profit health organizations and
world. Students will analyze structured and informal communication channels,          government health agencies, and community / public health education campaigns.
organizational culture, and strategic communication. Content includes                 In addition, the course will examine a variety of theories used in health
work-related human relations topics such as communicating gender, power               communication and explore such national health issues as health disparities, health
dynamics, mission, and identity. Other topics are conflict resolution, persuasive     literacy, advocacy and patient safety and healthcare reform. We will examine how
strategy, leadership, corporate culture, globalization, the role of technology, and   strategically designed and culturally grounded communication practices and
external communication such as crisis communication. Content includes the major       policies can be used to improve health outcomes. The course will examine multiple
theories of organizational communication, identifying and defining primary            levels of communication (intrapersonal, interpersonal, group, organizational, and
concepts and applying them to discussions of real-world situations. The theory and    societal communication), key human and mediated channels of communication, as

Global Health and Cultural Competency Certificate

Overview
Full Credential Title
Global Health and Cultural Competency Certificate

Catalog Description
The Global Health and Cultural Competency Certificate is designed to help
students in the Bachelor of Applied Science in Community Health Professional
program explore how culture influences healthcare delivery and outcomes.
Through field experience, students will apply their knowledge in real-world
settings, developing practical strategies to integrate cultural awareness into their
professional roles and enhance the quality of care they provide.

Total Number of Credits     ctcLink Plan Code            Effective Term
10                          PHGGHC01                     Fall 2025




Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Requirements
 CERTIFICATE COMPLETION REQUIREMENTS

          •   At least 25% of the applicable credits must be earned at Tacoma
              Community College.

                        ◦   Students who completed coursework at a college
                            other than TCC that they plan to apply to this degree
                            must submit an official transcript to Enrollment
                            Services for evaluation. All coursework must be from a
                            regionally accredited institution to be considered for
                            equivalency. For more information, visit Transfer
                            Credits to TCC or Enrollment Services in Building 7.

          •   A cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the coursework that is
              applied to the degree.

          •   After completing all certificate requirements, students must apply
              to graduate. Instructions to Apply to Graduate can be found here or
              by visiting Enrollment Services in Building 7.

methodological foundations and tools to study healthcare research. This course       General Information
will focus on the fundamentals of quantitative healthcare and applied research,      This course will compare and contrast healthcare delivery, to include the impact of
and explore qualitative research. Students will learn how to identify problems to    community health practice, in the United States with that of another country.

dependent variables, check for the validity and reliability of studies and design    values, and practices that are specific to that population in order to better
research projects. Students will be exposed to the broad range of designs used in    understand and provide health care that is both culturally competent and
healthcare research from laboratory and field experiments, surveys, content          culturally sensitive in nature. It introduces students to serving global populations
analysis, focus groups and in-depth interviewing.                                    both domestically and internationally as clinicians, educators, and researchers.

A Community Paramedic (CP) is an advanced paramedic that works to increase            Paramedic (CCP-C) exams held by the International Board of Specialty

CHRC430 - Advanced Patient Care

General Information
This comprehensive course gives Respiratory Care Practitioners the opportunity
to enhance their knowledge and critical thinking skills in the areas of adult critical
care, neonatal/pediatric critical care and emergency room environments. With
content geared toward respiratory critical care and general critical care, this
course is a comprehensive orientation to the respiratory therapists role in caring
for critically ill patients. This course prepares practitioners for specialty credentials
in adult critical care and neonatal/pediatrics.

Computed Tomography Department

Programs




                           164 / 478
Tacoma Community College




                                                                                         Program Map
Overview
                                                                                         Computed Tomography Certificate Program Map
Full Credential Title
                                                                                         Program maps are suggested academic plans and should not be used in the place of
Computed Tomography Certificate
                                                                                         regular academic advising. Your student entry method, placement, course
Catalog Description                                                                      availability, and program requirements are subject to change and transfer credit(s)
Students enrolled in the Computed Tomography (CT) Certificate program will fulfill       may change your individualized academic plan. Click here to connect with an
the professional requirements outlined in the ARRT Rules and Regulations.                advisor for this degree: Healthcare Pathways Advising
Successful completion of the program will qualify students to meet the eligibility
criteria for ARRT certification and registration as a Computed Tomography
Technologist. The program adheres to ARRT’s Computed Tomography Clinical
Experience Requirements, ensuring that all curriculum components and clinical
hours are properly documented for the certification and registration process.

The Computed Tomography program is part-time (1-2 classes per quarter) and is
five quarters total in duration. Classes will be held in-person with additional online
components, and clinical rotations will take place during the final quarter.

Admission to this program is not guaranteed upon general TCC admission.
Students must complete a secondary selective admission process. Students are
strongly encouraged to consult with an academic advisor and review the program’s
webpage for detailed information on application deadlines, selection criteria, and
required documentation.

This program requires that applicants provide documentation for one of the
following program prerequisites:

1. Radiologic Technologist RT(R) registry in good standing and WA state
Radiologic Technologist license in good standing; OR

2. Admission to TCC’s Radiologic Sciences program and completion of the first
two quarters of the program with a program average of a B or higher.

Total Number of Credits      ctcLink Plan Code           Effective Term
29                           MRTCTC20                    Fall 2025

              coursework that is applied to the certificate

Associate in Computer Science, DTA/MRP                                                 Composition II or Technical Writing or Oral Communication
                                                                                       Complete ALL of the following Courses:
Overview                                                                                        •   ENGL&102 - Composition II: Argument and Persuasion
Full Credential Title                                                                               OR ENGL&235 - Technical Writing
Associate in Computer Science, DTA/MRP                                                              OR CMST&220 - Public Speaking
                                                                                                    OR CMST&230 - Small Group Communication
Catalog Description
                                                                                                    OR CMST&240 - Intercultural Communication
The Associate of Arts in Computer Science (DTA/MRP) is designed for students
planning to transfer to a bachelor's program to major in Computer Science or a         Second Communication Skills course should be selected based on transfer
closely related field. In this program, students build strong foundations in           institution.
programming, mathematics, problem-solving, and computational thinking.                 Whitworth & Gonzaga- Oral Communication in place of English
Graduates are prepared for upper-division coursework and a range of careers in         Composition II or Technical Writing
software development, systems analysis, cybersecurity, data science, and related       Washington State University – Strongly recommend Technical Writing
fields.                                                                                instead of Composition II to meet degree requirements at transfer

Upon completion, students will be eligible to transfer with junior standing to most
Washington State baccalaureate institutions. Students transferring out of state
                                                                                      Quantitative/Symbolic Reasoning Skills (5 credits)

Courses
CS120 - Computer Science Principles                                                  CS142 - Java Programming for Engineers and Scientists I

General Information                                                                  General Information
Introduction to fundamental concepts of computer science and computational           Using the Java programming language, students learn general principles of
thinking. Course includes logical reasoning, problem solving, data representation,   object-oriented programming, including how to design, implement, document, test,
abstraction, the creation of digital artifacts such as Web pages and programs,       and debug computer programs. Topics include classes, objects, messages,
managing complexity, operation of computers and networks, effective Web              expressions, decision structures, iteration, arrays, collections, events, and
searching, ethical, legal and social aspects of information technology.              interfaces.

CS143 - Java Programming for Engineers and Scientists II

General Information
An intermediate programming course, using Java. Topics will include classes,
interfaces, inheritance, polymorphism, exception handling, recursion, data
structures, and an introduction to performance analysis and implementation
trade-offs.

The Diagnostic Medical Sonography program prepares students for employment                2025 or earlier.
as a Sonographer working in ultrasound imaging. It is a full-time, two-year program
(8 consecutive quarters, including two summer quarters) leading to an Associate in
Applied Sciences (AAS) degree. Upon graduation, students will be eligible to take
                                                                                         Degree Requirements
the ARDMS exams in the Abdomen and OB/GYN specialties.
                                                                                         Type
Students complete classroom and laboratory work at TCC and clinical education in         Completion Requirement
an affiliated ultrasound department. Clinical positions are assigned and often take
                                                                                         Additional Comments:
place in hospital ultrasound departments, clinics and private physicians' offices.

DMS106 - Sonography Lab II

General Information
This is the second of a four-part lab series (DMS 105, DMS 106, DMS 107 and DMS
108) designed to further develop and build on the foundations of sonography and
cross-sectional anatomy. This course introduces students to more complex
sonographic exams and patient care techniques. Students will learn how to apply
critical thinking and problem-solving skills, and hone interpersonal communication
techniques while working in a simulated healthcare setting. Activities will
emphasize the use of medical terminology (directional, anatomic, and sonographic),
anatomic relationships, scanning techniques and image optimization while focusing
on patient care and communication.

Components                               Credits
Lab                                      3

            • DMS105 - Fundamentals of Sonography + Lab I

                                                                                      DMS115 - Abdominal Sonography Extended (Small Parts
                                                                                      and Superficial Structures)

                                                                                      General Information
                                                                                      This course is an extension of Abdominal Sonography (DMS 114) and focuses on
DMS113 - Gynecology and Obstetrics I                                                  assessing anatomy, pathology, vascular abnormalities, and postoperative changes
                                                                                      of the abdomen, thorax, and superficial structures, including the thyroid and
General Information
                                                                                      scrotum. Students will learn and apply the fundamentals of investigating disease
This is the first of a three-part series (DMS 113, DMS 123, and DMS 126) that
                                                                                      processes by evaluating high risk populations, associating clinical indications, and
focuses on obstetrics and gynecology. This course will focus on the anatomy,
                                                                                      identifying pathologic features in sonographic images. Students will apply their
physiology, and sonographic appearance of gynecologic and first trimester
                                                                                      knowledge by identifying pathologic features in sonographic images, as well as how
obstetric structures. Clinical indications, imaging considerations and patient care
                                                                                      and when to communicate and expedite exam findings. Activities will emphasize
requirements specific to obstetric and gynecological patients will also be covered.
                                                                                      the use of medical terminology, problem-solving, communication and sonographic

DMS131 - Ultrasound Physics and Instrumentation II

General Information
This is the second of a two-part series (DMS 130 and DMS 131) focusing on the
mastery of sonographic physics principles. Advanced theory will be covered,
including Doppler, harmonic imaging, and pulse phasing techniques. Quality
assurance, ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable), bioeffects, safety
standards, and hemodynamics will also be covered. Students will complete their
study guides and prepare to sit for their first national registry exam, the
Sonography Principals and Instrumentation (SPI) exam.

Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   3

Courses
ECON&201 - Micro Economics

General Information
Theory of the market systems as a method of allocating resources and distributing
income and products. Analysis of current problems including government
regulation, subsidies, monopoly, and taxation.

Components                               Credits
Lecture                                  5

ECON&202 - Macro Economics

General Information
History and development of the economy of the United States economy, including
effects of government taxing and spending, control of the money supply, and
effects of international trade.

                                                                                                 •    EDUC&205 - Introduction to Education w/ Field Experience
The Associate in Arts Direct Transfer Agreement (DTA) is a statewide transfer
                                                                                                 •    EDUC220 - Diversity in Education
degree recognized by all Washington State public baccalaureate institutions and
                                                                                                 •    MATH&131 - Math for Elementary Education 1
many private colleges. It provides students with a broad foundation in the liberal
                                                                                                 •    MATH&132 - Math for Elementary Education 2
arts and is designed to support a seamless transition into bachelor’s degree
                                                                                                 •    PSYC&200 - Lifespan Psychology
programs.

The Elementary Education Specialization prepares students for careers in teaching
by introducing key concepts in child development, learning theory, and
                                                                                       College Success (5 credits)
instructional practices. This pathway is ideal for students intending to pursue a
                                                                                       Type
bachelor’s degree in education and ultimately become certified elementary
                                                                                       Completion Requirement
educators.

of state should consult with their intended institution to ensure course

The purpose of this course is to provide the student with an overview of the study       This introductory course will address supports and services across home and
of child development, including those children who are culturally, linguistically, and   community settings for culturally, linguistically, and ability diverse young children,
ability diverse. The scientific and theoretical approaches to studying the               ages birth to eight. Emphasis will be placed on identifying and using
development of the physical, cognitive, communication, social, and emotional             evidence-based practices for working with children with special needs and their
changes that occur from conception through adolescence and the biological and/or         families, and the resources and tools for navigating early intervention and special
environmental influences that affect this development will be addressed. Materials       education services, including the history, legislation, policies, and competencies
and resources regarding communication with families, brain development, and              that guide early intervention in the State of Washington. As part of this course, 40
milestones for each stage of development will also be provided. Four field               hours in a supervised Field Experience are required. The integration of theory and
observations within inclusive child care settings are required outside of class time.    developmentally appropriate evidence-based practice will be expected. Emphasis
                                                                                         will be placed on professional relationships, appropriate adult/child interactions,

EDUC220 - Diversity in Education

General Information
Examination of the relationship of cultural values to the formation of the child's
self-concept and learning styles. Examination of the role of prejudice, stereotyping
and cultural incompatibilities in education. Emphasis on preparing future teachers
to offer an equal educational opportunity to children of all socioeconomic and
cultural groups, as well as inclusion of exceptional children. (Multicultural Course)

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    5




EDUC299 - Early Childhood Field Experience

General Information
A 2-credit course based on an independent study contracted between an
instructor and a student. This class is taken concurrently with EDP-100 wich will
provide the opportunity to work with children in ECE classrooms, learning and
implementing developmentally appropriate practices in guidance, curriculum,
observation, assessment and the preparation of the physical environment.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    2

Catalog Description
The Paramedic Certificate program builds upon the Emergency Medical Technician          Fulfill ALL of the following requirements:
Certificate and is designed to prepare graduates to meet state certification
requirements and to take the National Registry EMT-Paramedic examination.                English Composition (5 credits)
Paramedics play a critical role in emergency medical services, acting as the eyes,       Complete ALL of the following Courses:
ears, and hands of an emergency physician in the field. Career opportunities                    • ENGL&101 - English Composition I
include positions with fire departments, hospitals, and private ambulance services.
Students can build upon this certificate to earn the Associate of Applied Science in
Emergency Medical and Health Services.                                                                                           AND

Admission to this program is not guaranteed upon general TCC admission.                  Quantitative Skills (5 credits)

                                                                                                       •    MATH&146 - Introduction to Statistics

required documentation. Before applying to the Paramedic program, students
must obtain their EMT certificate and have documentation of a minimum of 1 year
                                                                                                                                 AND
of active EMT experience (volunteer experience is acceptable).

                                                                                          EMC introduces the concept of pre-hospital care. Fundamental principles of the
General Information                                                                       practice are presented in this hybrid course, i.e., medical ethics, role &
This is a dynamic course that focuses on the various components of quality EMS.           responsibilities, critical thinking, medical math, and health and wellness.
The course utilizes assorted current readings in conjunction with small group work
                                                                                          Components                              Credits
that promotes critical thinking and a global understanding of what comprises state
                                                                                          Lecture                                 4
of the art out-of-hospital care.

skills, understanding the effects of different learning styles, producing strategies     concepts. Topics include: resistors, sources, capacitors, inductors, operational
for innovation, and fostering creativity in problem solving. Includes design projects,   amplifiers, node and mesh analysis, Thevenin and Norton equivalents, RLC circuits,
journal keeping, professionalism and ethical issues, and oral presentations.             phasors and steady state power in AC transmission. Solution of first and second
Acquaints students with disciplines and opportunities in engineering.                    order linear differential equations associated with basic circuit forms will be used.

General Information                                                                                 • PHYS&222 - Engineering Physics - Electricity and Magnetism
An introduction to Computer Aided Design (CAD) using software based on
parametric solid modeling. Students will use the software to create virtual models,
show the models in various projections and views, manage the associated
computer files, and produce engineering drawings. The course includes the
engineering graphics topics of three-dimensional visualization, sketching,
displaying solid objects in two-dimensional views, dimensioning, and reading

ENGR&215 - Dynamics                                                                    ENGR&225 - Mechanics of Materials

General Information                                                                    General Information
Dynamics of particles and rigid bodies, using the vector notation. Topics covered in   An introduction to the concepts of stress, strain, deformation, and failure theory in
this course include: kinematics, kinetics, rectangular coordinates, normal and         solid materials. Applies mechanics of materials concepts to structural and machine
tangent coordinates, polar coordinates, curvilinear motion, work, energy, impulse,     elements in tension, compression, bending, and torsion. Topics include deformation
momentum, impact, steady mass flow, rotation, absolute motion and relative             of members, Poisson's ratio, stress concentrations, thermal stress, statically
motion. Includes design project.                                                       indeterminate techniques, flexure formula, shear formula, stress transformation,
                                                                                       Mohr's circle, strain gauges, deflections, and columns. Includes a design project.
Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5                                            Components                                Credits
                                                                                       Lecture                                   5

ENGR&224 - Engineering Thermodynamics

General Information
Introduction to principles of thermodynamics from a predominantly macroscopic
point of view. Development of the basic laws of thermodynamics and their
application to energy transformation and state changes in engineering problems.
Topics include work, heat, energy, entropy, specific heat, open and closed system
analysis, and applications to devices and systems. Simple power and refrigeration
cycles, including Carnot, Otto, Diesel, Brayton and Rankine are introduced.

Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5

ELA76 - ELA Speaking and Listening Level 6

General Information
Students will learn to use a broad range of vocabulary and registers as well as
understand most details in conversations, short lectures, news reports and
extended explanations. Instruction will focus on building complex conversation
skills as well as developing strategies for repairing gaps in communication.

Components                  Credits                      Max Credits

ELA80 - Introduction to Practical Computing ELA

General Information
To provide English language learners with an opportunity to cultivate the computer
usage skills necessary to transition to college level courses as confident, successful
students. This course is an introduction to computer usage skills using the most
current Microsoft Windows operating system. Topics include: developing
computer literacy, navigation, organization, printing, file location and management,
accessing help.

Components                  Credits                      Max Credits
Lecture                     2                            10

FRSH75 - Reading and Writing for Social Studies: US
History

General Information
This theme based English course integrates reading, writing, listening, speaking,
and critical thinking skills around assignments and activities focusing on United
States History through multicultural perspectives. The course includes social and
political history and geography providing students with a broad view of Americas
past and present. Possible course topics include: African-American history,
immigrants, womens studies, Native American history, Civil Rights, and major
turning points in US history. This competency based class allows students to work
at their own pace, exit at a level appropriate to demonstrated skills and knowledge,
and earn possible high school completion English, Social Studies (US History and/or
Government and Civics), and/or elective credits.

FRSH82 - Applied Math I

General Information
Students will read, write and interpret basic mathematical information using whole
numbers, fractions, benchmark percents, and decimals. Students will be introduced
to basic patterns, data, algebraic concepts, measurement, geometry and
computational skills to solve 1-2 step contextualized real life word problems.

FRSH94 - Academic Reading and Writing II: Threshold                                FRSH98 - English - Fresh Start

General Information                                                                General Information
An integrated pre-college course designed to improve the student's reading and     Introduction to literacy and study skills for beginning students enrolled in the
writing ability for entrance into ENGL& 101. Course work focuses on critical       Fresh Start program. Academically, the focus is on developing competence in
reading and analytical writing in response to readings, with an emphasis on        Reading, English, and Technology, but instruction is also provided for social
organization, unity, coherence, and adequate development; an introduction to       development and is aimed at providing students with skills to be resilient and to
expository essays; and a review of the rules and conventions of standard written   focus on program completion.
English. Essay and research writing is included. This is a preparatory class for

college success with reading emphasis on text analysis for structure, inferring

meaning, critical thinking, and vocabulary development. Introduction to literary

                                                                                   This course is designed as an introduction to college for students entering the

FRSH95 - Intermediate Algebra

General Information
Topics include introduction to functions; linear, quadratic, exponential and
logarithmic functions and their applications; systems of linear equations and
inequalities and their applications; rational exponents and radicals.

  Additional Comments:                                                                 encouraged to select a specialization which will provide more focused

                                                                                                •   Associate in Science Transfer Track 2 - Atmospheric Science
                                                                                                    Specialization

                                                                                                •   Associate in Science Transfer Track 2 - Computer Engineering
                                                                                                    Specialization

                                                                                       In addition, there are four extended Engineering degrees, which require more than
                                                                                       90 credits to better prepare students to meet the prerequisites in their chosen
                                                                                       area of engineering.

                                                                                       The four Engineering degrees are:

                                                                                                •   Associate in Bioengineering and Chemical Engineering AS-T Track 2/
                                                                                                    MRP

                                                                                                •   Associate in Civil and Mechanical Engineering AS-T Track 2/ MRP

                                                                                                    Track 2/ MRP

                                                                                       PLEASE NOTE:
                                                                                       Many classes required for the Associate of Science Degree are offered only once or
                                                                                       twice a year. Students should work closely with Associate in Science advisors to
                                                                                       plan their programs of study, including elective courses.

                                                                                       This degree does NOT satisfy all General Education requirements of baccalaureate
                                                                                       institutions. Like students who begin their science studies at baccalaureate
                                                                                       institutions as freshmen, students who transfer with Associate of Science degrees
                                                                                       will typically be required to complete some general education requirements during
                                                                                       their junior and senior years.

                                                                                       Students pursuing this degree are strongly advised to contact their potential
                                                                                       transfer institutions early regarding the specific course choices in each area of the
                                                                                       degree. In addition, students should check with their potential transfer institutions
                                                                                       about minimum GPA requirements, possible higher minimum GPA requirements in
                                                                                       selected subsets of courses, and minimum grade requirements in specific courses.

         •   Elective courses should be selected based on major and transfer

                                                                               Sciences, Environmental/Resource Sciences, Chemistry,
                                                                               Geology, and Earth Science

                                                                               Overview
                                                                               Full Credential Title
                                                                               Associate in Science, Transfer Degree - Track 1: Biological Sciences,
                                                                               Environmental/Resource Sciences, Chemistry, Geology, and Earth Science

                                                                               Catalog Description
                                                                               The Associate in Science Track 1 (AS-T1) transfer degree is designed for students
                                                                               who plan to transfer to specific science majors at baccalaureate institutions.
                                                                               Students pursuing this degree are strongly encouraged to select a Track 1
                                                                               Specialization which will provide more focused requirements for students wishing
                                                                               to major in a specific area. These specializations meet the general Track 1
                                                                               requirements while providing major-specific coursework intended to prepare the
                                                                               student for transfer to a baccalaureate institution with junior standing.

                                                                                        •       Associate in Science Transfer Track 1 - Biology Specialization

                                                                                        •       Associate in Science Transfer Track 1 - Chemistry Specialization

                                                                                        •       Associate in Science Transfer Track 1 - Earth Sciences Specialization

                                                                                        •       Associate in Science Transfer Track 1 - Environmental Sciences
                                                                                                Specialization

                                                                               Students who are not working toward a specialization are discouraged from simply
                                                                               picking classes off the general Track 1 course list without the help of a TCC advisor
                                                                               who understands the Associate in Science requirements, as this may significantly
                                                                               extend the time required to earn a Bachelor’s degree.

                                                                               PLEASE NOTE:
                                                                               Many classes required for this degree are offered only once or twice a year.
                                                                               Students should work closely with Associate in Science advisors to plan their
                                                                               programs of study, including elective courses.

                                                                               This degree does NOT satisfy all General Education requirements of baccalaureate
                                                                               institutions. Like students who begin their science studies at baccalaureate
                                                                               institutions as freshmen, students who transfer with Associate in Science degrees
                                                                               will typically be required to complete some general education requirements during
                                                                               their junior and senior years.

                                                                               Students pursuing this degree are strongly advised to contact their potential
                                                                               transfer institutions early regarding the specific course choices in each area of the
                                                                               degree. In addition, students should check with their potential transfer institutions
                                                                               about minimum GPA requirements, possible higher minimum GPA requirements in
                                                                               selected subsets of courses, and minimum grade requirements in specific courses.

HIM465 - Health Compliance

General Information
This course will focus on organizational compliance related to regulation and
external forces that impact health information and quality of care. Students will
evaluate and manage standards, policies and procedures, quality improvement, and
risk management processes.

HIM485 - Capstone/Internship

General Information
This course includes the creation of a culminating capstone project and internship
to be completed in collaboration with a local health organization that will showcase
the students mastery of HIM domains. Additionally, each student will write and
present research findings and present prepare for the RHIA exam.

Components                                Credits

Catalog Description
The Health Information Technology (HIT) program prepares students for
                                                                                      First Quarter (15 credits)
entry-level career opportunities in the field of Health Information Management
(HIM) such as medical coding, release of information, or medical records              Earn a minimum grade of C in the following:
management. The HIT program is offered 100% online and leads to an Associate in                •     HIT120 - Information Protection
Applied Sciences (AAS) degree. Program curriculum includes disciplines of                      •     HIT125 - Record Content and Standards I
medicine, management, finance, information technology, and healthcare law.                     •     HIT126 - Health Information Technologies
Because of this unique mixture, graduates may work in a variety of work settings               •     LS101 - Introduction to Research
across an array of healthcare environments.

Graduates are eligible to sit for the Registered Health Information Technician
(RHIT) national credential exam. The program offers graduates direct articulation    Health Information Associate Certificate (28 Credits)
into TCC's Bachelor of Applied Science (BAS) in Health Information Management        Type
(HIM), which is also accredited by the Commission on the Accreditation for Health    Completion Requirement
Informatics and Information Management (CAHIIM). Graduates of the HIM BAS
                                                                                      Second Quarter (13 credits)
degree program are eligible to sit for the Registered Health Information
Management Administrator (RHIA) credential exam.                                      Earn a minimum grade of C in the following:
                                                                                              • MATH&146 - Introduction to Statistics

procedures. Insight will be gained into various stakeholders of the U.S. Healthcare     interoperability and consumer engagement activities.
System, external forces that impact healthcare, legislation and accreditation

HIT130 - Medical Terminology I                                                            HIT131 - Medical Terminology II

General Information                                                                       General Information
This course covers the study of roots, prefixes, suffixes; principles of word building,   This course covers the continuing study of selected roots, prefixes and suffixes;
study of diagnostic, operative, and symptomatic terms of body systems, emphasis           principles of word building; study of diagnostic, operative, and symptomatic terms
on accurate spelling and pronunciations; study of medical abbreviations, selected         of body systems; medical abbreviations; selected eponyms, and clinical laboratory
eponyms, clinical laboratory and radiology terminology. The course covers multiple        and radiology terminology. The course will focus on the integumentary,
systems with a focus on musculoskeletal, respiratory, and cardiovascular systems.         gastroenterology, neurology, urinary, male reproductive, gynecology, and
An emphasis is placed on accurate spelling and pronunciation of all medical terms.        obstetrics systems. There will be an emphasis on accurate spelling and
                                                                                          pronunciation of all medical terms.

HIT161 - Pathopharmacology                                                        HIT170 - Coding for Professional Services

General Information                                                               General Information
This course will cover the etiology, signs, symptoms, disease process, clinical   This course will introduce students to the basic principles and official guidelines of
course, diagnostic procedures, and pharmacology related to selected general       the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding Systems (CPT and HCPCS II) in various
medical conditions. Disease prognosis and therapeutic treatment will also be      healthcare settings. Students will gain an understanding of the CPT manual layout,
discussed.                                                                        coding rules and regulations, resources, Level II guidelines, and E/M coding
                                                                                  methods. Students will learn how to apply HCPCS chapter specific principles for
Components                                 Credits
                                                                                  code selection and sequencing in coding scenarios. Emphasis will be placed on the
Lecture                                    5
                                                                                  importance pairing diagnostic codes with HCPCS codes using medical
                                                                                  documentation and knowledge of human anatomy and pathophysiology to convey
                                                                                  medical necessity.

HIT195 - Health Law                                                                       HIT221 - Introduction to ICD-10-PCS/Inpatient Coding

General Information                                                                       General Information
This course discusses health law and policies that impact health information. Topics      This course will introduce students to the basic principles & official guidelines of
to be discussed include the United States legal system, federal and state legislation,    ICD-10-PCS and inpatient coding. Students will learn how to apply inpatient
policy-making processes, legal procedures, judicial processes, principles of liability,   coding guidelines and chapter specific principles for code selection and
confidentiality, informed consent, and release of information. Other topics to            sequencing. Emphasis will be placed on the importance of how to select
include the legal health record and designated record set, custodianship of the           ICD-10-PCS codes in the inpatient setting using medical documentation and
health record, retention and destruction of the health record, and health record          knowledge of human anatomy and pathophysiology.
archive methods.
