Overview
Full Credential Title                                                                           •   A cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the coursework that is
Associate in Arts, DTA - History Specialization                                                     applied to the degree.

Catalog Description                                                                             •   No more than 3.0 physical education activity credits will apply
The Associate in Arts Direct Transfer Agreement (DTA) is a statewide transfer                       toward the degree.
degree recognized by all Washington State public baccalaureate institutions and                 •   After completing all degree requirements, students must apply to
many private colleges. It provides students with a broad foundation in the liberal                  graduate. Instructions to Apply to Graduate can be found here or
arts and is designed to support a seamless transition into bachelor’s degree                        by visiting Enrollment Services in Building 7.
programs.

The History Specialization offers students a broad introduction to the political,        Specialization Requirements (30 credits)
social, cultural, and economic developments that have shaped American society            Type
and global civilizations from the past to the present. Through the study of historical   Completion Requirement
events, ideas, individuals, and movements, students gain a deeper understanding of
                                                                                          World Civilization Core Courses
how human societies have evolved and interacted across time.
                                                                                          Complete ALL of the following Courses:
This specialization prepares students to transfer with junior standing into a                       •   HIST&126 - World Civilizations I
bachelor’s degree program in History at most Washington State public                                •   HIST&127 - World Civilizations II
baccalaureate institutions. Students planning to transfer to institutions outside                   •   HIST&128 - World Civilization III
Washington should consult with their intended transfer school to ensure course
compatibility and transferability.
                                                                                          U.S. History Core Courses
Students are strongly encouraged to work closely with an academic advisor to
                                                                                          Complete ALL of the following Courses:
develop an education plan that meets both degree requirements and the specific
                                                                                                    •   HIST&146 - US History I
admission criteria of their intended transfer institution. Some universities or
                                                                                                    •   HIST&147 - US History II
education programs may require additional prerequisites, a minimum GPA, or a
                                                                                                    •   HIST&148 - US History III
competitive application process.

  Multicultural Course (5 credits)
  Type
  Completion Requirement

    Multicultural Course

    A single course may be used to satisfy the multicultural requirement, one
    specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement. Please
    consult the listings under each distribution area requirement to identify
    courses that fulfill both criteria.

    For the History Specialization, students may apply a single
    Multicultural-designated course from the Social Sciences Distribution to
    fulfill up to three requirements simultaneously - multicultural
    requirement, one specialization requirement, and one distribution
    requirement.

    This overlap does not reduce the total degree requirement of 90 quarter
    credits.

    Earn at least 5 credits from the following:
             • Multicultural Courses

    Recommended Courses:
    HIST&126 World Civilizations I - World Civilizations I (History
    Specialization Course, Social Science Distribution, & Multicultural Course)
    HIST&127 World Civilizations II - World Civilizations II (History
    Specialization Course, Social Science Distribution, & Multicultural Course)

    Specialization Course, Social Science Distribution, & Multicultural Course)

HIST&219 - Native American History                                                       HIST210 - History of Modern Europe

General Information                                                                      General Information
This course will examine the history of Native American cultures in the United           An introduction of the common heritage, global dynamics, and historical
States. Through readings, lectures, and class discussions, students will explore the     components that crafted the making of modern Europe. The course will assess the
unique histories of several specific Native cultures, as well as the impact that these   unfolding historical events from the 17th century up to the present, examining
cultures had in shaping the course of American history. (Multicultural Course)           major societies in the region, including British, French, German, Italian, and Russian
                                                                                         societies, among others. The focus will be on the interactions among Europe's
Components                                 Credits
                                                                                         different societies, and between Europe and the larger world. (Multicultural
Lecture                                    5
                                                                                         Course)

HIST244 - The 1960s

General Information
This course will examine key political, social, cultural, economic, and diplomatic
trends in American society from 1960 to 1974. Topics covered will include John F.
Kennedy and the "New Frontier," Lyndon Johnson's "Great Society," the civil rights
movement, the women's movement, the war in Vietnam, the anti-war movement,
and Richard Nixon and Watergate. Particular focus will be on the individuals,
issues, ideas and events most responsible for shaping this period and our
understanding of it.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    5

HFL141 - Child and Family Studies II - Child Development

General Information
This course will have an emphasis on the development of children's social skills,
values and goals through communication and guidance techniques. Understanding
what behaviors to expect at various ages; learning the language to build
self-esteem; positively communicating expectations and limits and problem solving
techniques. Students in this course should meet college-level expectations.

Components                                 Credits

HD105 - Career and Life Planning

General Information
This course is designed to help students explore their unique interests, values,
skills, capabilities, strengths, and achievements to learn about themselves and find
a career path that is in alignment. Youll learn how to effectively present and
articulate your experiences, skills, interests, goals, and values to potential
opportunities, and cultivate the resources and capital necessary to aid in your
personal and professional development. Well engage in practical and experiential
opportunities, as well as self-reflection and assessment activities to ultimately
develop plans to prepare you for an ever-changing world of work.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    3

the course examines the variations in an individual's path through grief and loss, as
                                                                                        Enrollment Requirements

HSP224 - Dynamics of Family Violence                                                       Earn a minimum Grade of C in the following:
                                                                                                   • HSP100 - Introduction To Human Services
General Information
This course explores the history, philosophy and present status of violence in the
family; examines cultural and social perspectives regarding violence across the life
span including child mistreatment, sexual abuse, and elder maltreatment; and
exposure to service delivery systems specific to family violence.

HSP293 - Supervised HSP Internship III

General Information
This final clinical practicum experience again requires 116 hours of supervised
work. The focus on HSP 293 is on integration of field experience with classroom
learning to form a personal theory of helping. Weekly seminars will alternate
between discussing issues that arise from the field and refining helping strategies
as well as helping students to synthesize theories and techniques into a working
model.

Components                                Credits
Lecture, Clinical                         5




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

  Prerequisite

    Human Services Program
    Earn a minimum Grade of C in the following:
            • HSP292 - Supervised HSP Internship II

HUM285 - The City

General Information
An in-depth study covering history, philosophy, religion, art, architecture and
literature of a culturally significant city. A different city, time, and place will be
studied each quarter Humanities 285 is offered.

Catalog Description
The Associate in Applied Science (AAS) in Networking and Cybersecurity prepares
students for entry-level careers in network administration, technical support,
database management, and cybersecurity. The curriculum combines technical core       General Education Requirements (15 credits)
courses that emphasize hands-on experience with systems, data management,            Type
networking, and security principles, alongside non-technical core courses that       Completion Requirement
develop essential business, project management, and communication skills.
Advanced coursework includes training in database administration, Windows and         Composition Course
Linux server environments, network infrastructure, routing protocols,                 Complete ALL of the following Courses:
cybersecurity practices, and virtualization technologies.                                      •   ENGL&101 - English Composition I

                                                                                      The Bachelor of Applied Science in Advanced Networking and Data Security is
Program Map                                                                           designed to prepare students for leadership roles in the information technology
Associate in Applied Sciences in Networking & Cyber Security Program Map              field. This program emphasizes both technical proficiency and essential soft skills,
Program maps are suggested academic plans and should not be used in the place of      equipping graduates to become well-rounded IT professionals capable of thriving
regular academic advising. Your student entry method, placement, course               in dynamic, team-oriented environments.
availability, and program requirements are subject to change and transfer credit(s)
                                                                                      Grounded in ethical principles, the curriculum integrates advanced training in
may change your individualized academic plan. Click here to connect with an
                                                                                      cloud computing, cybersecurity and digital forensics, network infrastructure, and
advisor for this degree: Business, Paralegal, and Technology Pathway Advising.
                                                                                      database management. Students gain hands-on experience configuring and
                                                                                      maintaining current and emerging technologies for diverse business and
                                                                                      government applications.

                                                                                      Through a comprehensive, interdisciplinary approach, the program fosters critical
                                                                                      thinking, effective communication, and collaborative problem-solving. Graduates
                                                                                      are prepared to pursue industry-recognized certifications and meet the evolving
                                                                                      demands of the IT workforce with a competitive edge and a versatile skill set.

                                                                                      Admission to this program is not guaranteed upon general TCC admission.
                                                                                      Students must complete a secondary selective admission process. Students are
                                                                                      strongly encouraged to consult with an academic advisor and review the program’s
                                                                                      webpage for detailed information on application deadlines, selection criteria, and
                                                                                      required documentation.

Technical Support Certificate                                                        Program Map
                                                                                     Technical Support Certificate Program Map
Overview                                                                             Program maps are suggested academic plans and should not be used in the place of
Full Credential Title                                                                regular academic advising. Your student entry method, placement, course
Technical Support Certificate                                                        availability, and program requirements are subject to change and transfer credit(s)
                                                                                     may change your individualized academic plan. Click here to connect with an
Catalog Description
                                                                                     advisor for this degree: Business, Paralegal, and Technology Pathway Advising.
The Technical Support Certificate program builds upon foundational PC support
and networking concepts, providing students with the opportunity to develop
advanced skills in network configuration, diagnostics, and administration.
Graduates are prepared to take the Network+ certification exam. Students must
complete the Help Desk Certificate or have equivalent work experience as
determined by the program chair before completing this certificate.

Total Number of Credits     ctcLink Plan Code           Effective Term
35                          CSSTSC20                    Fall 2025




Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Requirements
          •   At least 25% of the applicable credits must be earned at Tacoma
              Community College.

                        ◦   Students who completed coursework at a college
                            other than TCC that they plan to apply to this degree
                            must submit an official transcript to Enrollment
                            Services for evaluation. All coursework must be from a
                            regionally accredited institution to be considered for
                            equivalency. For more information, visit Transfer
                            Credits to TCC or Enrollment Services in Building 7.

          •   A cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the coursework that is
              applied to the degree.

          •   After completing all certificate requirements, students must apply
              to graduate. Instructions to Apply to Graduate can be found here or
              by visiting Enrollment Services in Building 7.

General Information                                                                  General Information

system architecture, system boards, expansion cards, memory, input/output            various types of problems as preliminary design tools, and then will design, code,
devices, and peripherals. The student learns how to build, configure, and            test, and debug programs using a programming language.
troubleshoot a personal computer, and install its operating system. The objectives

IT322 - Forensics I                                                                         Instructor Permission

                                                                                            Prerequisite: Instructor permission required
General Information
Provides in-depth foundation in data recovery and computer forensics. Covers
legal issues relevant to information security and digital forensics professionals.
Students will learn the proper way to gather and secure evidence, document a
computer crime scene, retrieve lost files, retrieve deleted files and e-mails and
reconstruct data from various computer storage media. Students learn use of
                                                                                        IT418 - Advanced Technology Integration
media imaging and data recovery tools. Stresses proper documentation and
                                                                                        General Information
procedural guidelines. Students get hands-on experience with some of the popular
commercial and open source digital forensics tools.                                     This class covers data and communication technologies whose development, and
                                                                                        practical application is either newly emerging or whose potential is largely

ITC100 - Introduction to Microcomputers

General Information
Introduction to Microcomputers course is designed to provide a basic computer
skill set and understanding of computer functions. These computing skills may
serve useful in personal computing and as productivity tools for entry level
occupational computer use. Students will learn digital computer concepts,
computer hardware, peripheral devices, Windows operating system,
wordprocessing, electronic spreadsheets, and email applications.

Components                               Credits
Systems                                  5

                                                                                   JAPN&123 - Japanese III

                                                                                   General Information
                                                                                   JAPN&-123 is the third quarter of the first-year language sequence and continues
                                                                                   to build on the skills acquired in JAPN&-122. The basic tenets of communications
                                                                                   including reading, writing, speaking, and listening are developed in a dynamic
                                                                                   second language acquisition environment enhanced by technology. Grammar is
                                                                                   used as a communication tool to express meaning as students take part in small
                                                                                   group discussions, cultural investigation, and presentation. Authentic materials will
                                                                                   be focused on in the class as well as assessment. Students should expect to be
                                                                                   immersed in the language. English will be used at a minimum. This course satisfies
                                                                                   the Multicultural distribution requirement. (Multicultural Course)

                                                                                         LOG108 - International Trade and Logistics Management

                                                                                         General Information
                                                                                         This course introduces students to import and export management and ties this to
                                                                                         the requisite logistics management issues, including dealing and communicating
                                                                                         with customers, suppliers, freight companies, banks, and government agencies.
                                                                                         Students will learn about necessary policies, processes, documentation and
                                                                                         compliance procedures, and will study a variety of viable strategies for
                                                                                         international market entry.

LOG115 - Logistics Security and Risk Management

General Information
This course overviews logistics security threats and risks and the countermeasures
that can be used to secure fixed assets and assets in transit. Students will become
familiar with applicable domestic and international standards, laws, and
regulations as well as the technologies that are used in ensuring that the supply
chain is safe.

Lecture                                    3

MATH&131 - Math for Elementary Education 1                                               General Information
                                                                                         In-depth study of the concept of a function, including graphs, transformations,

whole numbers, fractions, decimals and their operations, and proportion and              prepare students for Calculus. Graphing calculator required.
percentage. Emphasizes deep conceptual understanding, problem solving, and

MATH238 - Elements of Differential Equations

General Information
Introductory Course in ordinary differential equations. Topics includes first- and
second-order differential equations, Laplace transform, power series solutions,
applications and modeling. Graphing calculator required.

Catalog Description                                                                                                 of 90 quarter credits.
The Associate in Arts Direct Transfer Agreement (DTA) is a statewide transfer
degree recognized by all Washington State public baccalaureate institutions and                  •   A cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the coursework that is
many private colleges. It provides students with a broad foundation in the liberal                   applied to the degree.
arts and is designed to support a seamless transition into bachelor’s degree                     •   No more than 3.0 physical education activity credits will apply
programs.                                                                                            toward the degree.
The Digital Music Specialization offers students the experiences to achieve                      •   After completing all degree requirements, students must apply to
competencies in modern recording technology, music production and                                    graduate. Instructions to Apply to Graduate can be found here or
performance. Students focus on learning the digital tools needed in the audio/                       by visiting Enrollment Services in Building 7.
music profession, and also get experiences in several key areas of the music
profession: songwriting, sound management, and performance.

The Associate in Music, DTA/MRP is a state-recognized Direct Transfer Agreement         Type
/ Major Related Program (DTA/MRP) designed for students who plan to pursue a            Completion Requirement
Bachelor of Music degree in performance, composition, theory, history, music
education, or music therapy. The program provides comprehensive preparation in           Communication Skills (10 credits)
music theory, performance, and musicianship while fulfilling general education           Fulfill ALL of the following requirements:
requirements for transfer at most Washington baccalaureate institutions.

Washington State baccalaureate institutions. Students transferring out of state
                                                                                                 • ENGL&101 - English Composition I
should work closely with the transfer institutions to understand how courses in
this degree will transfer.

General Information

MUSC&142 - Music Theory II
                                                                                                   • MUSC&142 - Music Theory II
General Information
Introduction to the study of Western diatonic harmony. Analysis of 18th and 19th
century harmonic usage; composition exercises stressing correct voice-leading and
chord succession. Further development of aural skills through ear-training and
sight-singing.                                                                         MUSC&241 - Music Theory IV
Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5
                                                                                       General Information
                                                                                       The study of chromatic harmony in Western music. Analysis of 18th and 19th
                                                                                       century harmonic usage; continued secondary chord functions and temporary
                                                                                       harmonic modulations. Introduction to large-scale binary and ternary form and
Enrollment Requirements                                                                analysis. Further development of aural skills through ear training and sight singing.

MUSC&242 - Music Theory V                                                             MUSC110 - Introduction to Digital Music

General Information                                                                   General Information
The study of extended chromatic harmony. An analysis of 18th and 19th century         This course introduces students to using computers and digital audio work station
harmonic usage; modulations, extended chromatic chord structures, and mode            applications to make music. Topics covered include: an applied understanding of
mixture. Continued study of binary and ternary forms and analysis. Further            the elements of music, basic audio editing techniques, basic recording techniques,
development of aural skills through ear training and sight singing.                   basic MIDI sequencing, and live performance with digital technology.

Components                                Credits                                     Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5                                           Lecture                                   5

MUSC106 - World Music

General Information
An introduction to musics of various cultures of the world. This class explores the
elements of music, music as an expression of human values, music as an identity,
and music as a lifestyle. (Multicultural Course)

Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5

Tacoma Community College




MUSC125 - Class Applied Music: Piano II                                            MUSC131 - Applied Lessons Strings 1

General Information                                                                General Information
Second class in a sequence of instruction for the beginning piano student.         Private instruction on a string instrument (violin, viola, cello, bass, harp, guitar).

                                                                                                                                                                370 / 478
Tacoma Community College




MUSC297 - Music Specialization Capstone

General Information
This course provides the culminating experience(s) for students completing the AA
Music Specialization and AA Digital Music Specialization. Students work closely
with their professor to complete a wide variety of capstone projects in completion
of their degree. Possible capstone projects could include: professional internship,
creation of professional resume, creation of electronic press kit, development of
professional website, creation of digital portfolio, distribution of recordings,
creation of a business plan, and more.


This course is required as part of the AA Digital Music Specialization.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    3




MUSC299 - Independent Study

General Information
Independent learning activity designed jointly by student and instructor to
improve and/or increase the learner's knowledge and skill in the area of music. This
course is an elective, and transferability is inconsistent. Instructor approval needed
for enrollment. Variable credit course.

Components                  Credits                      Max Credits
Lecture                     1                            5

NURS116 - Skills and Assessment Lab II                                                NURS153 - Pharmacology I

General Information                                                                   General Information
This course introduces the nursing student to the application of concepts through     This course introduces the student to pharmacology. Pharmacological concepts
clinical skills in the laboratory setting. Concepts of assessment, caring             included in this course are: pharmacokinetics, routes of administration, dosage

SOCSC204 - Psychosocial Issues in Healthcare 1

General Information
This course examines concepts necessary for the effective provision of healthcare
in the psychosocial domain. It addresses determinants of health and illness across
the lifespan, including social, psychological, environmental, spiritual, and cultural
dimensions. Concepts include communication, family, culture & diversity,
spirituality, caring interventions, development, stress and coping, and self.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    3

General Information
This course examines concepts necessary for the effective provision of healthcare
in the psychosocial domain. It addresses determinants of health and illness across
the lifespan, including social, psychological, environmental, spiritual, and cultural
dimensions. Concepts include cognition, violence, mood and affect, behavior, and
stress and coping.

                                                                                         385 / 478
Tacoma Community College




Programs
Certified Nursing Assistant Certificate

Overview
Full Credential Title
Certified Nursing Assistant Certificate

Catalog Description
The Nursing Assistant Certified (NAC) program prepares students for employment
as Certified Nursing Assistants (CNAs) in a variety of healthcare settings, including
hospitals, long-term care facilities, and home health environments. CNAs work
under the supervision of licensed nursing professionals, providing direct patient
care and essential support. They play a critical role in ensuring patient comfort,
safety, and well-being, while also maintaining accurate documentation of the care
provided.

The curriculum includes comprehensive training in key areas such as patient rights,
communication and interpersonal skills, safety and emergency procedures, CPR
certification, basic nursing and personal care techniques, restorative services, and
support for individuals experiencing life transitions.

Graduates of the program will be eligible to take the Nursing Assistant
Certification (NAC) Exam, which includes both an in-person skills assessment and
an online written test.

The Nursing Assistant Certified (NAC) program at TCC is approved by the State of
Washington, Department of Health, and Nursing Care Quality Assurance
Commission.

Admission to this program is not guaranteed upon general TCC admission.
Students must complete a secondary selective admission process. Students are
strongly encouraged to consult with an academic advisor and review the program’s
webpage for detailed information on application deadlines, selection criteria, and
required documentation.

Total Number of Credits       ctcLink Plan Code           Effective Term
10                            NAACNC01                    Fall 2025




Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Requirements
 CERTIFICATE COMPLETION REQUIREMENTS

            •   All 10 credits for this certificate must be earned at Tacoma
                Community College.

            •   All required courses must be completed with a grade of C or higher
                to count towards certificate completion.

            •   A cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the coursework applied
                to the certificate.


  Certificate Requirements
  Type
  Completion Requirement

    Core Courses
    Complete ALL of the following Courses:
                •   NAC110 - Fundamentals of Patient Care
                •   NAC120 - Patient Care Clinical
                •   NAC198 - Foundations of Nursing Assistant Care

NUTR250 - Nutrition in Healthcare I

General Information
An introduction to the science of nutrition. Examines the foundational concepts of
diet and nutrition in relation to health across the lifespan and in the context of
healthcare professions.

Oceanography Department
For more information on our Oceanography department: Oceanography : Tacoma Community College (tacomacc.edu)




                                                                                                                                                                 388 / 478
Tacoma Community College



Oceanography Department Chair: Heather Cushman - Hcushman@tacomacc.edu

Courses
OCEA&101 - Introduction to Oceanography

General Information
Focuses on topics such as the origin of ocean basins, seawater chemistry,
atmospheric and oceanic circulation, waves, and marine ecosystems. The
laboratory includes investigations of oceanographic phenomena and local field
trips.

Components                                Credits
Lecture, Lab                              5




Enrollment Requirements
Simple Requisites

 Course Requirements

   Recommended Preparation
   Type
   Prerequisite

    Recommended
    Proficiency in Algebra.




OCEA179 - Special Topics in Oceanography

General Information
These are seminars designed to provide Oceanography students with the
opportunity to study selected oceanography topics and techniques. Topics and/or
techniques covered will generally be determined by the instructor but may also
reflect the expressed need or interest of students in the earth science program.

Components                    Credits                 Max Credits
Lecture                       1                       5




OCEA299 - Independent Study in Oceanography

General Information
Independent study of oceanographic topics.

Components                    Credits                 Max Credits
Lecture                       1                       5

Tacoma Community College




Paralegal Studies Department
TCC’s American Bar Association-approved program offers options to those who wish to pursue a career in the exciting, fast-growing field of legal services. Graduates may be
eligible for positions in a law office, legal department of a corporation, public agency, court system, private investigation firm, and paralegal services. Legal courses are taught
by faculty who represent various aspects of the field of law and hold a high regard for academic and professional integrity.


Webpage: https://www.tacomacc.edu/academics-programs/programs/paralegalaas
Department email: PLST@tacomacc.edu
Department Chair: Beth Acken - Backen@tacomacc.edu

Courses
PLST150 - Paralegal Fundamentals and Ethics                                           PLST151 - Legal Research and Writing I

General Information                                                                   General Information
Students will learn about the professional development and career opportunities       An introduction to state and federal legal research practices and legal writing.
in the paralegal profession. Students will increase their understanding of specific   Emphasis is on the location and analysis of validated, primary legal sources,
areas of the law, the sources of American law, and the American court system.         including statutes and cases. Students will practice writing professional legal
Students will also examine the ethical and professional responsibilities, the         documents, such as client correspondence, case briefs, and legal memorandums,
professional organizations, and the skills needed for a paralegal career.             using proper legal citations.

Components                                Credits                                     Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5                                           Lecture                                   5

PLST152 - Introduction to Civil Law                                                        PLST153 - Civil Procedure I

General Information                                                                        General Information
An overview of the civil litigation process, civil rules of procedure for Washington,      An overview of the civil litigation process, civil rules of procedure for Washington,
local court rules, and the types of cases handled in civil court. This includes an         local court rules, and the types of cases handled in civil court. This includes an
examination of the pre-litigation process, the commencement of a civil action,             examination of prelitigation, the commencement of a civil action, pleading and
pleading and motions, parties to a case, discovery of evidence, trial, judgments,          motions, parties to a case, discovery of evidence, trial, judgments, appeals, and
appeals, and alternative dispute resolution options. Students will examine the             alternative dispute resolution options. Students will examine the paralegal’s role in
paralegal’s role in this process. Students will draft civil litigation documents with an   this process. Students will draft civil litigation documents with an emphasis on
emphasis on using the Washington State Court Rules, as well as civil litigation case       using the Washington State Court Rules, as well as civil litigation case law,
law, effectively.                                                                          effectively.

PLST156 - Criminal Procedure for Paralegals                                              PLST157 - Legal Technology

General Information                                                                      General Information
This course is an overview of criminal law with an emphasis on Washington                Students will increase their understanding and use of technology that is relevant in
statutes and criminal procedure from arrest to final disposition and appeals. The        the paralegal profession. Students will understand and use netiquette in the
course emphasizes the constitutional rights of the accused and the role paralegals       development of legal documents and communications, and the use of video
play in the criminal justice system. Students will practice writing documents            conferencing systems. Students will be familiar with current electronic case
typically drafted by paralegals in a law office or agency in the field of criminal law   management systems, court filing systems, and financial management systems.
defense or prosecution.                                                                  Students will also examine the ethical and professional responsibilities required of
                                                                                         attorneys when using legal technology.

                                                                                         Lecture                                   5

PLST201 - Interviewing and Investigations                                             PLST221 - Family Law

General Information                                                                   General Information
Students are introduced to the legal contexts in which interviewing and               To provide an overview of legal authorities and procedures involved in family law.
investigating skills are utilized. This includes the introduction to factual          Topics include marriage and domestic partnership rights and agreements;
investigations and interviewing, with emphasis on methods of investigation, role of   dissolution of marriage and domestic partnerships; spousal support and
pretrial discovery, examination of evidence, use of experts, obtaining public and     categorization of property; child custody, visitation, and support; adoptions and
private information, and the ethical considerations associated with interviews and    parenting issues; and domestic violence. The skills necessary for a paralegal
investigations. Great emphasis is placed on the development of communication          working in family law will be emphasized.
skills for interviewing and the use of resources available for investigating,
                                                                                      Components                                Credits
particularly Internet resources.

estate planning. Topics include estate planning tools such as wills and trusts;      or trial. Topics include negotiation, mediation and arbitration, as well as other

tax consequences; and health care directives. The skills and document production     Washington State.
necessary for a paralegal working in the area of probate and estate law will be

PLST224 - Real Estate Law                                                          PLST226 - Administrative Law

General Information                                                                General Information
An introduction to law and legal systems as related to real estate transactions.   Study of administrative procedures acts governing state and federal agencies.
Major topics include forms of ownership; title, transfers and insurance; fraud,    Topics include creation of agencies, constitutional limits on agencies, agency rule
deceit and misrepresentation; contract law and documents; real estate security     making and authority to act, scope of judicial review, and legal procedures for
documents; and duties and liabilities of licensees. Emphasis is placed on the      administrative law cases.
paralegal’s role in real estate and property law practice.
                                                                                   Components                                Credits
Components                                Credits                                  Lecture                                   3

PLST237 - Introduction to Tax Law                                                        PLST251 - Legal Research And Writing II

General Information                                                                      General Information
An introduction to federal income tax law. Following traditional instruction             Building on the research and writing skills acquired in PLST 151, emphasis is on
methodology supplemented with practical law office skill development, this course        critical analysis of legal authorities, analytical reading and writing, and persuasive
outlines theories behind federal income tax law, identifying various sources of          and objective writing techniques. Students prepare a variety of documents
taxation authority, observing financial circumstances that may determine tax             requiring citation format for cases, statutes, and secondary legal authority,
liability, legal and ethical implications, and organizing and preparing a client's tax   including a motion with persuasive brief.
information for use in preparing necessary taxation forms.

PHYS&223 - Engineering Physics - Waves, Optics, and
Thermodynamics

General Information
Waves, Sound, Light and Thermodynamics. Calculus based physics for engineering
or those majoring in certain sciences. Introduces problem solving techniques and
applications to real world situations. Topics include oscillations, damping, waves,
interference, refraction, reflection, optical systems, fluids, specific heat,
temperature, ideal gasses, and states of matter. Laboratory included.

POLS&101 - Introduction to Political Science                                            POLS&202 - United States Government

General Information                                                                     General Information
This course is intended to serve as a comparative introduction to the concepts,         This course is intended to provide a basic understanding of the American political
methods, and subject areas of the discipline of political science. Students will        system. We will examine the structural foundations of American politics, including
explore various forms of political beliefs, behaviors, institutions, and processes at   our constitutional framework, political culture, and political economy. We will then
the individual, group, national, and international levels. Strongly recommended for     explore the various political institutions which serve as a link between citizens and
those students with no prior coursework in political science.                           government, including public opinion, the mass media, interest groups, social
                                                                                        movements, political parties, and elections. Following this, we will turn our
Components                                 Credits
                                                                                        attention to the branches of the federal government, including Congress, the
Lecture                                    5
                                                                                        Presidency, the federal bureaucracy, and the Supreme Court.

POLS&201 - Introduction to Political Theory                                               Additional Comments:
                                                                                          or concurrent enrollment in ENGL&101
General Information
This course provides a survey of the underlying philosophical ideas and ideologies
in the subfield of political science known as political theory. Some of the topics to
be considered include essentially contested concepts like democracy, freedom,           POLS&203 - International Relations
community, rights, citizenship, and justice which provide the philosophical
foundation for various competing political ideologies like classical and welfare        General Information
liberalism, conservatism, and socialism as well as contemporary debates on issues
                                                                                        This course is intended to serve as an introduction to the concepts, methods, and
such as political extremism, environmentalism, feminism, human rights, and              subject areas of the field of international relations within the discipline of political
multiculturalism.                                                                       science. Students will explore various forms of political beliefs, behaviors,
Components                                 Credits                                      institutions, and processes shaping the relationships between states and other
Lecture                                    5                                            non-governmental actors in the international system.

POLS240 - Environmental Politics and Sustainability

General Information
This class examines the study of politics and policies surrounding sustainability
including definitions of sustainability; individual, local, state, national, and
international solutions to environmental problems; economics; environmental
justice; and business practices.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    5

Overview
Full Credential Title                                                                          •   A cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the coursework that is
Associate in Arts, DTA - Psychology Specialization                                                 applied to the degree.

Catalog Description                                                                            •   No more than 3.0 physical education activity credits will apply
The Associate in Arts Direct Transfer Agreement (DTA) is a statewide transfer                      toward the degree.
degree recognized by all Washington State public baccalaureate institutions and                •   After completing all degree requirements, students must apply to
many private colleges. It provides students with a broad foundation in the liberal                 graduate. Instructions to Apply to Graduate can be found here or
arts and is designed to support a seamless transition into bachelor’s degree                       by visiting Enrollment Services in Building 7.
programs.

The Psychology Specialization offers students a strong foundation in the scientific     Psychology Specialization Requirements (30 credits)
study of human behavior and mental processes. This is approached through an             Type
intersectional lens that considers how culture, identity, and social structures shape   Completion Requirement
individuals and societies.
                                                                                         Psychology Core Courses (15 credits)
This specialization prepares students to transfer into bachelor's degree programs        Complete ALL of the following Courses:
in psychology and related disciplines at most Washington State public                              •   PSYC&100 - General Psychology
baccalaureate institutions, while also developing critical thinking, analytical, and               •   PSYC202 - Biopsychology
communication skills applicable to a variety of fields. Students planning to transfer              •   PSYC209 - Fundamentals of Psychological Research
to institutions outside Washington should consult with their intended transfer
school to ensure course compatibility and transferability.

                                                                                                   •   PSYC&200 - Lifespan Psychology

competitive application process.

  Multicultural Course (5 credits)
  Type
  Completion Requirement

   Multicultural Course

   A single course may be used to satisfy the multicultural requirement, one
   specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement. Please
   consult the listings under each distribution area requirement to identify
   courses that fulfill both criteria.

   For the Psychology Specialization, students may apply a single
   Multicultural-designated course from the Social Science Distribution to
   fulfill up to three requirements simultaneously - multicultural
   requirement, one specialization requirement, and one distribution
   requirement.

   This overlap does not reduce the total degree requirement of 90 quarter
   credits.

   Earn at least 5 credits from the following:
            • Multicultural Courses

Courses
PSYC&100 - General Psychology                                                        PSYC&200 - Lifespan Psychology

General Information                                                                  General Information
Introduction to psychology as an academic discipline, including research methods,    An examination of the physical, social, emotional, and intellectual development of
the nervous system, heredity and development, social behavior, motivation,           the human from conception to death through a multicultural lens. A scientific
emotion, learning and memory, cognition, personality, and psychological disorders.   approach is applied, which aims to explain growth, change, consistency and
                                                                                     variations in development through the lifespan. Topics covered include heredity/
Components                               Credits
                                                                                     epigenetics, sex and gender development, socialization influences/ parenting,
Lecture                                  5
                                                                                     trauma, identity formation, mid-life challenges, and practices surrounding death
                                                                                     and dying. (Multicultural) (Writing Intensive)

                                                                                     Components                                Credits
Enrollment Requirements
                                                                                     Lecture                                   5

PSYC&180 - Human Sexuality
                                                                                     General Information
General Information
                                                                                     Thsi course provides an integrative approach to understanding the development
A scientific approach to the study of human sexuality and sexual behavior,
                                                                                     and symptoms of mental disorders. Students explore the psychological, biological
including physiological and psychological components. Considers sexuality across
                                                                                     and sociocultural factors that contribute to mental illness, with an emphasis on
the lifespan, sexual dysfunction and therapy, STDs and safer sex, LGBT issues,
                                                                                     theories of abnormal behavior. Clinical assessment, diagnosis and treatment of the
pregnancy and contraception, communication and relationships.
                                                                                     major mental disorders of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental
Components                               Credits                                     Disorders-5th edition (DSM-5) are explored. Some of the topics that are covered
Lecture                                  5                                           include depression, bipolar disorder, anxiety disorders, personality disorders and
                                                                                     schizophrenia. Recommended for students interested in counseling or clinical
                                                                                     psychology.

PSYC092 - Job Search Skills                                                                PSYC209 - Fundamentals of Psychological Research

General Information                                                                        General Information
A Department of Corrections Offender Change course focused on re-entry to help             This course covers the key themes and concepts of psychological research and is a
prepare offenders for successful integration into communities upon release from            foundation course for students planning to take additional courses in psychology.
prison. Students focus on the process of finding employment.                               Topics include hypothesis testing, experimental design, research strategies and
                                                                                           techniques, fundamentals of scientific writing, search and evaluation of research
                                                                                           literature in psychology, and ethical issues in psychological research.
                                                                                           (Writing-Intensive Course)
PSYC202 - Biopsychology
                                                                                           Components                                 Credits
General Information                                                                        Lecture                                    5
Biopsychology examines the biological basis of behavior, the nervous system, how
it works to control behavior and sense the world, and what happens when it
malfunctions. Topics include learning and memory, development, sex, drugs, sleep,
                                                                                           Enrollment Requirements
the senses, emotions, and mental disorders. Course is intended for Psychology
                                                                                           Simple Requisites
majors intending to transfer, especially to UW-Seattle or to the BS program at PLU.

Components                                  Credits                                         Course Requirements
Lecture                                     5

PSYC299 - Individual Study in Psychology

General Information
A variable credit (1-3) course based on independent study contracted between an
instructor and a student. The emphasis will be a research related project which will
provide an opportunity for students to pursue in-depth in an area previously or
concurrently covered in a college-level course.

Components                   Credits                       Max Credits
Lecture                      1                             3

    Psychology
    Prerequisite: PSYC& 100 with a minimum grade of B+ AND Instructor
    permission




PSYC301 - Fundamentals of Research for Healthcare

General Information
This course will provide the student with an introduction to research methods as
well as analysis and presentation of data. Topics will include a statistical analysis of
healthcare data, descriptive, inferential, and vital statistics, data reporting and
presentation techniques, research design and methods, and the use of Institutional
Review Boards.

Components                                  Credits
Lecture                                     5

Associate in Applied Science in Radiologic Science
                                                                                      Additional Comments:
Overview                                                                                      • A minimum of 114 quarter credit hours in courses numbered
                                                                                                  100 or above.
Full Credential Title
Associate of Applied Science in Radiologic Science                                            •       At least 25% of the applicable 114 credits must be earned at
                                                                                                      Tacoma Community College.
Catalog Description
TCC's Radiologic Science program prepares students for employment as                          •       A combined cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the
Radiologic Technologists and leads to an Associate in Applied Sciences (AAS)                          coursework that is applied to the degree.
degree. Clinical practice is performed in program affiliated hospitals and clinic
imaging centers. TCC’s Radiologic Science program is in Diagnostic Radiography
only. The program courses are offered sequentially and students should plan to        Core Requirements
attend full time.                                                                     Type
                                                                                      Completion Requirement
Graduates of the Radiologic Science program are eligible to apply to take the
national certification examination administered by the American Registry of            Quarter 1 (17 credits)
Radiologic Technologists. Successful completion of the registry examination results
                                                                                       Complete ALL of the following Courses:
in national certification as a Registered Radiologic Technologist, RT (R) ARRT.
                                                                                              • RS100 - Radiologic Science Orientation
Admission to this program is not guaranteed upon general TCC admission.                           •    RS108 - Radiation Physics I
Students must complete a secondary selective admission process. Students are                      •    RS140 - Radiographic Positioning I with Simulation
strongly encouraged to consult with an academic advisor and review the program’s                  •    RS170 - Fundamentals of Patient Care
webpage for detailed information on application deadlines, selection criteria, and
required documentation.                                                                Quarter 2 (16 credits)

This course introduces students to the Radiologic Science Program. Students learn         This course introduces the concepts of electromagnetic radiation, its production,
and interpret the program's mission and goals, professional requirements, and             control, and interactions. The concepts of electricity, electromagnetism, and
commitment necessary for success. The students will apply understanding of how            electrical circuits relating to imaging equipment operation are presented.This
to operate the lab equipment to better prepare them for instruction. This course          course will focus on radiation physics and instrumentation.
will implement, present, and prepare students for the clinical arena through
                                                                                          Components                                Credits
modules created by our affiliates to gain entry to their clinical rotation. This course
                                                                                          Lecture, Lab                              4
will introduce student resources available for virtual and on-campus support.

RS101 - Fundamentals of Radiologic Science and Health
Care

General Information
Content is designed to provide a foundation of the principles, practices and policies
in healthcare and particularly radiology. The student will be introduced to the
history of radiology, medicolegal ethics and professional associations. Radiation
protection and safety, basic equipment operation, and principles of radiographic
exposure will also be covered.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    4

Associate in Applied Science in Respiratory Therapy                                                       For details, visit Enrollment Services - Transfer Credits to

Catalog Description
The Respiratory Therapy program prepares students for employment as                      Complete ALL of the following Courses:
respiratory care practitioners . The program is full time and sequential, consisting            • BIOL&241 - Human Anatomy and Physiology 1
of seven instructional quarters offered during daytime hours. Students complete                      •    BIOL&242 - Human Anatomy and Physiology 2
classroom and laboratory course work on campus and gain clinical experience in                       •    HIT130 - Medical Terminology I
affiliated clinical sites.                                                                                OR HIT105 - Comprehensive Medical Terminology
Graduates of the Respiratory program are eligible to take the Therapist                              •    MATH&146 - Introduction to Statistics
Multiple-Choice Examination (TMC) administered by the National Board for                             •    PSYC&100 - General Psychology
Respiratory Care (NBRC). The TMC exam is designed to objectively measure                                  OR PSYC&200 - Lifespan Psychology
essential knowledge, skills, and abilities required of entry-level respiratory
therapists, as well as determine eligibility for the Clinical Simulation Examination.   Additional Comments:
There are two established cut scores for the TMC exam. If a candidate achieves the
lower cut score, they will earn the Certified Respiratory Therapist (CRT) credential.
If a candidate achieves the higher cut score, they will earn the CRT credential AND     Degree Requirements
become eligible for the Clinical Simulation Examination to earn their Registered        Type
Respiratory Therapist (RRT) credential. Currently, the CRT is the minimum               Completion Requirement
credential required for licensure in 40 of the 49 states (Alaska doesn't require        Additional Comments:
licensure at this time). The remaining 9 states, including Washington, require the               •       A minimum of 109 quarter credit hours in courses numbered
RRT credential. Graduates are encouraged to earn their advanced credential (RRT)                         100 or above.
as more and more states are moving to RRT as the minimum requirement for
licensure.                                                                                       •       At least 25% of the applicable 109 credits must be earned at
                                                                                                         Tacoma Community College.
To practice, respiratory therapists must be licensed by the state as respiratory care
practitioners. Licensure requirements typically include completing an accredited                 •       A combined cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the
training program, passing a background check, and obtaining the Certified                                coursework that is applied to the degree.
Respiratory Therapist (CRT) and/or Registered Respiratory Therapist (RRT)
credential from the National Board for Respiratory Care (NBRC).

                                                                                         First Quarter (Fall)
webpage for detailed information on application deadlines, selection criteria, and
required documentation. Admission is competitive and there are usually more              Complete ALL of the following Courses:

prerequisite and co-requisite courses and meet the other application requirements               • RC150 - Fundamentals of Respiratory Care I
are not guaranteed program admission.                                                           • RC160 - Applied Cardiopulmonary Anatomy and Physiology

RC151 - Fundamentals of Respiratory Care II

General Information                                                                   RC153 - Alternative Procedures in Respiratory Care
Course will continue to build on the clinical skills and knowledge base acquired in
RC 150. The Respiratory Care student will be introduced to the critical care          General Information
environment this quarter. Emphasis is placed on airway management, diagnostic         This course will provide students with knowledge regarding special procedures
testing and non-Invasive ventilation.
                                                                                      they will be assisting physicians in performing as well as introducing additional
Components                                Credits                                     skills based upon Respiratory Care Scope of Practice. Information presented will
Lecture                                   4                                           build upon previously studied pathophysiologies as well as set-up, procedural skills
                                                                                      and processing of specimens obtained when applicable. The course will be a

RC290 - Ethics and Professionalism in Respiratory Care

General Information
While providing care and delivering therapies, RCP's must be continuously aware
of the legal and ethical implications of the services they deliver and their actions
while delivering them. This course will provide students with information
necessary for understanding legal issues and for making ethical decisions in
respiratory care.

Components                                 Credits
Lecture                                    2

Courses
SCI105 - Introductory Topics in Natural Science

General Information
Concepts from multiple scientific fields (including biology, chemistry, physics, and
the earth and space sciences) will be investigated through the in-depth exploration
of an applied interdisciplinary topic. This course will provide introductory-level
science students with an expansive understanding of scientific principles, methods,
and techniques. Laboratories and field work included.

The Associate in Arts Direct Transfer Agreement (DTA) is a statewide transfer                      toward the degree.
degree recognized by all Washington State public baccalaureate institutions and                •   After completing all degree requirements, students must apply to
many private colleges. It provides students with a broad foundation in the liberal                 graduate. Instructions to Apply to Graduate can be found here or
arts and is designed to support a seamless transition into bachelor’s degree                       by visiting Enrollment Services in Building 7.
programs.

The Sociology Specialization offers students a strong foundation in the key             Sociology Specialization Requirements (25 credits)
concepts, terminology, and contemporary developments in the dynamic field of            Type
sociology. A background in sociology supports a wide range of career paths,             Completion Requirement
including social services, community outreach, education, public policy, human
                                                                                         Core Courses
resources, and entry-level research roles in both public and private sectors.

SOC&201 - Social Problems
                                                                                   SOC200 - Introduction to Research Methods for
General Information                                                                Sociologists
A macro-sociological approach to the study of social problems with special
emphasis on the effects of institutional change in the economic, educational,      General Information
family, political, religious, and other systems of contemporary human societies.   This course is an introduction to research methods in sociology, covering
(Multicultural Course)                                                             quantitative, qualitative, primary and secondary data and defining the basic types
Components                                Credits                                  of research method including social surveys, experiments, interviews, focus groups,
Lecture                                   5                                        participant observation, ethnography and longitudinal studies. This course will also
                                                                                   review general issues in the design and implementation of research projects, as
                                                                                   well as specific issues that arise in conducting interviews and field observations,
                                                                                   constructing and administering surveys, analyzing existing data, and planning
Enrollment Requirements                                                            program evaluations.

SOC299 - Individual Study

General Information
A variable credit (1-5) course based on independent study contracted between an
instructor and a student. The emphasis will be a research related project which will
provide an opportunity for students to pursue in-depth in an area previously or
concurrently covered in a college-level course.

SPAN&123 - Spanish III

General Information
SPAN&-123 is the third quarter of the first-year language sequence and continues
to build on the skills acquired in SPAN&-122. The basic tenets of communications
including reading, writing, speaking, and listening are developed in a dynamic
second language acquisition environment enhanced by technology. Grammar is
used as a communication tool to express meaning as students take part in small
group discussions, cultural investigation, and presentation. Authentic materials will
be focused on in the class as well as assessment. Students should expect to be
immersed in the language. English will be used at a minimum. (Multicultural
Course)

Components                                Credits
Lecture                                   5

WRITE141 - Writing Center Tutor Practicum II

General Information
Provides qualified students with training and experience in assisting students in
one-on-one tutorial sessions at the Writing & Tutoring Center. Topics of study
include advanced tutoring topics such as working with at-risk students. Course
includes regular tutor discussion group meetings and activities in Angel. Students
will apply tutoring skills in actual sessions and have the opportunity to earn the
second level of International Tutoring Program Certification.

Components                                 Credits
Systems                                    2

WRITE142 - Writing Center Tutor Practicum III

General Information
Provides qualified students with training and experience in assisting students in
one-on-one and group tutorial sessions at the Writing & Tutoring Center. Includes
the opportunity to hold a tutoring leadership role. Course includes regular tutor
discussion group meetings and activities in Angel. Students will apply tutoring skills
in actual sessions and have the opportunity to earn the third level of International
Tutoring Program Certification.

  Multicultural Requirement (5 credits)
  Type
  Completion Requirement

   Multicultural Course
   A single course may be used to satisfy the multicultural requirement, one
   specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement. Please
   consult the listings under each distribution area requirement to identify
   courses that fulfill both criteria.

   For the Interdisciplinary Writing Specialization, students may apply a
   single Multicultural-designated course from the Communication Skills,
   Social Sciences, or Humanities Distribution to fulfill up to three
   requirements simultaneously - multicultural requirement, one
   specialization requirement, and one distribution requirement.

   This overlap does not reduce the total degree requirement of 90 quarter
   credits.

   Earn at least 5 credits from the following:
            • Multicultural Courses

Overview
Full Credential Title                                                                             •   A cumulative grade point average of 2.00 in the coursework that is
Associate in Arts, DTA - Literature Specialization                                                    applied to the degree.

Catalog Description                                                                               •   No more than 3.0 physical education activity credits will apply
The Associate in Arts Direct Transfer Agreement (DTA) is a statewide transfer                         toward the degree.
degree recognized by all Washington State public baccalaureate institutions and                   •   After completing all degree requirements, students must apply to
many private colleges. It provides students with a broad foundation in the liberal                    graduate. Instructions to Apply to Graduate can be found here or
arts and is designed to support a seamless transition into bachelor’s degree                          by visiting Enrollment Services in Building 7.
programs.

 The Literature Specialization provides a solid introduction to the concepts,             Literature Specialization Requirements (25 credits)
terminology, interpretation and analysis of literature. This specialization prepares      Type
students for successful transfer to a four-year college at the junior level in English,   Completion Requirement
American Studies, or Literature. TCC's Literature Specialization includes a rich
                                                                                           Core Course
variety of exciting courses ranging from ancient to postmodern literature and from
the traditional to the experimental or avant-garde. Students work closely with             Complete ALL of the following Courses:
accomplished faculty who are published poets, novelists, and nonfiction writers.                      •   ENGL&244 - American Literature I

Graduates are prepared to transfer to bachelor’s programs with junior standing to
most Washington State baccalaureate institutions, having satisfied most or all             Special Focus Courses
lower-division general education requirements. Students planning to transfer out           Complete at least 4 of the following courses:
of state should consult with their intended institution to ensure course                              •   ENGL&220 - Introduction to Shakespeare
transferability. Students are strongly encouraged to work closely with an academic                    •   ENGL&226 - British Literature I
advisor to develop an education plan that meets both degree requirements and the                      •   ENGL&227 - British Literature II
specific admission criteria of their intended transfer institution. Some universities                 •   ENGL234 - Introduction to Mythology and Folk Stories
or education programs may require additional prerequisites, a minimum GPA, or a                       •   ENGL242 - Contemporary Non-Western Literature
competitive application process.                                                                      •   ENGL&245 - American Literature II

ENGL234 - Introduction to Mythology and Folk Stories                               ENGL261 - The Bible as Literature

General Information                                                                General Information
A comparative study of myths and folklore throughout the world. Emphasis is on     The Bible as Literature examines the Bible, both the Hebrew Bible and the
the commonality of concerns and values expressed by both western and               Christian Testament, as a collection of literary works. Students learn to recognize
non-western cultures and on the literary value of myths and folklore.              plot, theme, character, setting, style, and figurative language and to consider the
                                                                                   interaction of those elements. They also learn about the history of the composition,
Components                               Credits
                                                                                   transmission, and translation of the works. There is emphasis on the original
Lecture                                  5
                                                                                   cultural context of the stories and on the ways they have been revised and
                                                                                   reinterpreted in other cultures. The purpose of the course is not to supplant the
                                                                                   Bible as a religious text, but to enhance each student's appreciation of the Bible
Enrollment Requirements                                                            regardless of his or her religious convictions.

ENGL242 - Contemporary Non-Western Literature

General Information
A survey of contemporary non-Western literature. Various cultural and historical
themes will be studied from quarter to quarter.
                                                                                   ENGL262 - Children's Literature
Components                               Credits
Lecture                                  5                                         General Information
                                                                                   Children's Literature is the study of classic and contemporary fiction and poetry for
                                                                                   children, from the early reading levels through high school. Although the course is
Enrollment Requirements                                                            intended for students who plan to become teachers, it is open to all students who
Simple Requisites                                                                  meet the prerequisites. Students examine and critique a variety of works on the
                                                                                   basis of the elements of literature, such as plot, character, theme, setting, and style.
 Course Requirements                                                               They research the historical contexts of the works. They present their critiques and
                                                                                   findings in written form, along with specific suggestions for teaching the works.
